games.explanation: The market is looking at a bad White Sox team on the road and forgetting to ask who's actually pitching for the other side, which happens to be Bryan Hudson and a walk rate that belongs in a bullpen phone booth. Hudson has run a 15.5% walk rate in his big-league work, and asking him to navigate Kurtz and Langeliers without free passes is asking a lot. Chicago's righty bats travel fine against this arm profile, and the wind blowing out at 13.5 mph doesn't do a command-shaky lefty any favors either. Getting a coin-flip price on the team the numbers make a clear favorite is the entire ballgame.

Cam Schlittler has spent the year looking sharper against lefties than righties, which is inconvenient news for the Yankees when Washington's lineup is stacked with left-handed bats who happen to punish exactly the pitch mix he leans on. James Wood against right-handed pitching is the centerpiece here, and Abrams and García behind him are the same story with less thunder and more contact. Yes, Mikolas is the worse arm on paper, but the market has priced this like a mismatch when the projected score is closer to a one-run coin flip. Getting +165 on the team the numbers make a slight favorite is the kind of arithmetic error you cash and don't overthink.

Getting a plus price on the better team is the whole ballgame, and the market has this one backwards because it sees the Mets logo and the Peralta strikeout numbers and stops reading. Peralta is a different pitcher against lefties, where his walk rate balloons and the whiffs dry up, which is a problem when Abreu and the lefty side of this Boston order is exactly who's waiting for him. Boston is also just the sturdier club by a wide Elo margin, and the sim has this as a coin flip that the board has priced like a mismatch. At +128 on the team more likely to win, you don't need to overthink it.

A coin-flip game shouldn't be priced like a coin flip when one side is getting plus money, and that's the entire pitch here. Reynaldo Lopez has been a fly-ball arm in a park that plays fair, and the Cardinals happen to run out the kind of right-handed contact bats (Herrera in particular) who don't need much help to do damage against him. Liberatore isn't an ace, but his walk rate stays under control and Atlanta's lineup outside of Baldwin has been closer to replacement-level than the name value suggests, with Dubon and Albies both sitting under a .300 xwOBA on the year. Getting the better team on our simulation at +103 is the kind of price the market rarely leaves sitting out this long.

Yamamoto is the better pitcher, nobody's arguing that, but +230 is pricing this game like the Diamondbacks are showing up with a Little League roster. Arizona's top four all swing right-handed and Yamamoto is meaningfully more hittable from that side, where his barrel rate against jumps to nearly 9%. Marte and Carroll are exactly the kind of hard-contact bats that turn a tidy Yamamoto start into a messy one, and a desert night with the wind blowing out doesn't do the Dodgers any favors either. Pfaadt is the reason this line exists, but the market is charging you full freight for a coin that lands heads maybe 60% of the time. At +230, you only need to be right on the underdog about three times in ten.

picks.explanation: Joe Ryan is a fine pitcher having a good year, but the market is treating him like he tames left-handed bats when the opposite is true, and the Angels happen to run four of them through the top of the order. Trout, Schanuel, and Soler have all done real damage against righties this year, and Ryan's splits against lefties (a .305 xwOBA and a strikeout rate that drops nearly ten points) say the whiffs he lives on don't show up tonight. Ryan Johnson is the wild card, but the projected margin here is well inside a run, which is all a plus-1.5 ticket needs. Getting a runner and a half on a coin-flip game priced at -130 is the entire argument.

Nobody expects the Nationals to win this game, and the pick isn't asking them to. Schlittler is a fine young arm but he's more hittable against righties than the reputation suggests, and Washington's best bat happens to be a left-handed masher who eats that exact profile for breakfast. Mikolas is the obvious anchor here, except the Yankees do most of their damage on barrels and Washington's ballpark quietly suppresses the long ball just enough to matter. Getting a plus price on the runline in a game the sims see finishing within a run is the whole pitch, and at +100 the market is basically conceding that this isn't the blowout the moneyline implies.

Getting a plus price on the better team is the whole ballgame, and the market has this one backwards because it sees the Mets logo and the Peralta strikeout numbers and stops reading. Pfaadt is the reason this line exists, but the market is charging you full freight for a coin that lands heads maybe 60% of the time. Getting a run and a half with a plus number against a Yamamoto team feels wrong until you remember what plus-one-five actually asks: don't get blown out. Yamamoto is legitimately excellent, but he's meaningfully more hittable against righties, and Arizona's top four all swing from that side with Marte carrying a .390 xwOBA into the box. Pfaadt is the obvious drag on the ticket, except the projected margin is under a run and this park inflates homers enough that one Carroll or Marte swing rewrites the math. At +105, you're being paid to bet that a good Dodgers team wins a normal baseball game instead of a laugher.
